name: master italian green sauce and pesto
instructions:
A progression of italian pasta sauces built around green herbs and vegetables — read in REVERSE order compared to the tomato master. Where the tomato master starts simple and builds up to the raw pesto, the green master starts at the raw pesto (variant A, the canonical simple mortar version) and moves outward toward a more complex blended sauce that borrows pesto DNA but adds cream, canned tomatoes, and extra vegetables (variant B). This inversion tracks the actual pattern of each family: the traditional italian tomato dishes are cooked sauces; the traditional italian green dish is the raw pesto. In each case A is the classic canonical form and B/C are the extensions. For tomato-based italian sauces (Hazan's 3-ingredient, halfway-pesto, sicilian pesto rosso), see master-italian-tomato-sauce-and-pesto.

=== VARIANT A — CLASSIC MORTAR PESTO (BARE-BONES) ===

The canonical simple form. Made in a mortar and pestle. Soft ingredients only — the mortar can't break down anything fibrous or hard.

INGREDIENTS:
- fresh basil (canonical); baby arugula (mixed greens) or spinach also work as soft-green alternatives
- pine nuts (canonical); cashews or sunflower seeds also work as soft-nut alternatives
- garlic (raw, 1 clove — see notes on managing potency)
- olive oil (good quality)
- grated parmesan cheese
- black pepper
- salt to taste
- pasta

PROCEDURE:
1. In a mortar, pound garlic with a pinch of salt into a paste.
2. Add the greens and nuts. Pound to a rough paste — the goal is a coarse texture, not smooth.
3. Drizzle in olive oil while stirring with the pestle until you get a loose, oily paste.
4. Fold in grated parmesan and black pepper. Salt to taste.
5. Toss with cooked pasta. Optional finish: a splash of heavy cream for a richer read, or fresh chopped tomatoes for brightness.

=== VARIANT B — COMPLEX BLENDED SAUCE (PESTO-ADJACENT) ===

Departs from the pestle canon and moves into blender territory. Ingredient palette is inclusive — fibrous greens, hard nuts, canned tomatoes, and heavy cream all belong here in ways they don't in variant A. Closer to a chunky pasta salad than traditional pasta-with-sauce.

INGREDIENTS:
- basil PLUS any combination of: parsley, kale, asparagus, carrot greens, spinach, mixed greens
- any nuts: pine nuts, almonds, cashews, sunflower seeds (mix and match)
- garlic (2-3 cloves — blender handles more than mortar)
- olive oil
- heavy whipping cream (canonical liquid component) OR lemon juice (lighter version)
- canned tomatoes (a structural primary here, not a garnish)
- grated parmesan cheese
- black pepper
- yellow onion (optional; briefly sautéed before blending — see notes)
- salt to taste
- crunchy finish: slivered almonds or sunflower seeds folded in AFTER blending (toasted better)
- pasta — PENNE or other short/tubular shapes, NOT spaghetti

PROCEDURE:
1. If using yellow onion, sauté briefly in a small pan first to soften the raw bite.
2. Combine greens, nuts, garlic, olive oil, heavy cream (or lemon juice), canned tomatoes, sautéed onion, and black pepper in a blender or food processor.
3. Blend until reasonably smooth but with some texture — this isn't a smooth sauce, it's a chunky rebuild.
4. Fold in grated parmesan and salt to taste (fold in by hand, don't blend the cheese).
5. Toss with cooked penne. Fold in the crunchy nut/seed finish on top.
6. Loosen with pasta water very sparingly — see notes.
notes:
THE PROGRESSION — CANONICAL RAW PESTO → COMPLEX BLENDED SAUCE (REVERSE OF THE TOMATO FAMILY): the green sauce family runs in reverse of the tomato family. Traditional italian green cooking centers on the raw mortar pesto (variant A); traditional italian tomato cooking centers on the cooked sauce (variant A over there). So the ladder here starts at the canonical simple form and moves outward toward a more inclusive, cooked-liquid, tomato-and-cream-adjacent version. Reading them in this order helps you see what each variant is doing and when to reach for which.

=== VARIANT A NOTES ===

PESTLE RULES — SOFT THINGS ONLY: the mortar can't break down fibrous or hard ingredients. That's the primary constraint that shapes the whole variant.
- GREENS: basil, baby arugula (mixed greens), spinach are the soft leaves that pestle well. Parsley does NOT work — too fibrous, doesn't break down by hand.
- NUTS: pine nuts (canonical), cashews, sunflower seeds all pestle. ALMONDS do NOT work — too hard, you need a blender or food processor. Almonds are for variant B or for red pesto.
- TOMATOES: if adding them, fresh only — finely chopped and rinsed, as a secondary addition. Canned tomatoes are wrong texture for a mortar dish.

GARLIC IN PESTLE PESTO IS POTENT: raw garlic pounded into a paste is much more aggressive than raw garlic in a blender-smoothed sauce. Two ways to manage:
- Use sparingly — one clove per batch is usually enough.
- Pre-macerate the garlic in lemon juice for a few minutes, then drain aggressively before pestling. Softens the raw garlic bite; the trap is leaving too much juice and making the pesto too lemony.

CREAM IS OPTIONAL BUT WELCOME AS A FINISH: a splash of heavy whipping cream stirred in at the end of variant A works as a richer finish. Not traditional but fine — it's the bridge into variant B territory.

RAISINS OPTIONAL — SICILIAN-LEANING: a small handful of raisins works as a sweet-savory move against the pine-nut + basil base. Sicilian tradition.

MORTAR RATHER THAN BLENDER FOR TEXTURE AND FLAVOR: pesto made in a mortar has less air incorporated and preserves the basil's oils better — smoother mouthfeel, deeper flavor, less oxidized color. A food processor works in a pinch but the result reads flatter.

=== VARIANT B NOTES ===

WHAT VARIANT B IS FOR — INCLUSIVE, VEGETABLE-DENSE, RICHER: variant B is when you want more vegetables, more fiber, more nuts, and a creamier read than the pestle can produce. It's still a green sauce (basil-forward) but reads closer to a chunky pasta salad than traditional pasta-with-sauce.

GREENS ARE INCLUSIVE — MIX AND MATCH: basil works, but so do parsley, kale, asparagus, carrot greens. Spinach and mixed greens fine. The blender breaks down fibrous things the pestle can't.

ALL NUTS WORK — INCLUDING ALMONDS: the blender can pulverize any of them. Almonds, pine nuts, cashews, sunflower seeds — mix and match.

VEGETABLES AND FIBER ARE WELCOME: blender pesto loses fiber vs pestle, so adding more vegetables (asparagus, kale, carrot greens, yellow onion) restores nutritional density and body. This is one of variant B's real advantages.

HEAVY CREAM IS LOAD-BEARING (OR LEMON JUICE): the blender needs a liquid component to blend smooth. Heavy cream is canonical here and part of what makes variant B feel richer; lemon juice can stand in for a lighter version. Don't rely on olive oil alone — the result reads greasy rather than rich.

CANNED TOMATOES SHINE HERE: where fresh tomatoes were the only tomato option in variant A, canned tomatoes are structural in variant B. The concentrated flavor integrates with the green pesto base after blending. Primary ingredient, not a substitute.

CRUNCH AT THE END — SLIVERED NUTS FOLDED IN AFTER BLENDING: restores some of the chew you lose by blending everything smooth. Slivered almonds or sunflower seeds work best; toasted better than raw.

ONION HANDLING: yellow onion in the blender works but the raw bite can be harsh. Sauté briefly before blending to soften the edge.

BLENDER FAILURE MODES (LEARNED THE HARD WAY):

- TOO MUCH PASTA WATER AT THE END: unlike the pestle version, the blender base already carries lots of liquid (cream, lemon, canned tomatoes). Adding pasta water — the standard pasta-finishing move — can instantly ruin the dish here. Use a fraction of what you'd add to a regular pasta. Err on the dry side and add water tablespoon by tablespoon.

- SPAGHETTI INSTEAD OF PENNE: blender pesto is chunky, blended-and-rebuilt — closer to a pasta salad than traditional pasta-with-sauce. It needs a pasta shape that holds chunks and picks up sauce in pockets. Penne (or other tubular/short shapes) is the right call. Spaghetti slides under the chunky sauce structure and reads wrong.

- TOO MUCH PROTEIN (NUT/SEED OVERLOAD): the blender makes it tempting to keep adding nuts to thicken the sauce. Past a certain point the dish swings PROTEIN SHAKE — gritty, dense, one-note. If the sauce is too thin, add more canned tomato or reduce — don't add more nuts.

=== SHARED / CROSS-VARIANT NOTES ===

STORE-BOUGHT BASIL PESTO IS CLASHING IN BOTH VARIANTS: jarred pesto isn't good enough for either technique. The pestle has texture and freshness a jar can't match; the blender has volume and inclusion logic a jar wasn't built for. Don't substitute.

BASIL IS THE STRUCTURAL GREEN IN BOTH — DON'T DROP IT: variant A can flex to spinach or arugula, and variant B can add kale/parsley/asparagus alongside, but basil is the through-line for both. Without basil the dish stops reading as pesto and starts reading as a generic green sauce.

SHORT PASTA IN BOTH VARIANTS: pesto (either variant) prefers short pasta shapes. Long strands don't grip the sauce. Penne is the workhorse for both.
